text: Jean-Paul Sartre's existentialist maxim {{c1::'existence precedes essence'}} states that humans exist first, and then define their purpose and identity through their {{c1::choices and actions}}.
explanation: There is no pre-defined human nature or destiny. We are completely free and responsible for who we become, which can cause existential dread (angst).
usage: You are not born a 'failure' or a 'hero'; you construct your identity daily through the commitments you choose to keep.
spanish: La máxima existencialista de Jean-Paul Sartre 'la existencia precede a la esencia' establece que los humanos existen primero y luego definen su propósito a través de sus elecciones.

text: Immanuel Kant's supreme moral principle that one should act only according to maxims that can be universalized is the {{c1::Categorical Imperative}}.
explanation: The Categorical Imperative asserts that moral duties are absolute and binding regardless of consequences. Act only on rules you would want everyone in the world to follow without contradiction.
usage: Under Kantian ethics, lying is always morally wrong, even to protect someone, because universalized lying destroys the trust required for language.
spanish: El principio moral supremo de Immanuel Kant de que uno debería actuar únicamente de acuerdo con máximas que puedan universalizarse es el Imperativo Categórico (Categorical Imperative).

text: Friedrich Nietzsche posited that the fundamental driving force of all organic life is not self-preservation but the {{c1::Will to Power}} (Der Wille zur Macht).
explanation: The Will to Power represents the drive to master oneself, expand influence, dominate obstacles, and achieve self-overcoming, contrasting with Darwinian survival metrics.
usage: Nietzsche used the Will to Power to criticize conventional Christian morality as life-denying.
spanish: Friedrich Nietzsche postuló que la fuerza motriz fundamental de toda vida orgánica no es la autoconservación sino la Voluntad de Poder (Will to Power).

text: Jean-Paul Sartre's core existentialist premise that humans define their own purpose and nature through choices is summarized as {{c1::existence precedes essence}}.
explanation: Unlike objects (which are designed with a pre-defined essence/purpose), humans are born first (exist) without any inherent purpose. We must construct our own values and meaning through our actions.
usage: Because existence precedes essence, humans are 'condemned to be free' and carry absolute responsibility for their lives.
spanish: La premisa existencialista central de Jean-Paul Sartre de que los seres humanos definen su propio propósito y naturaleza a través de las elecciones se resume como la existencia precede a la esencia.

text: Michel Foucault analyzed how modern institutions enforce compliance and self-surveillance using Jeremy Bentham's architectural design of the {{c1::Panopticon}}.
explanation: The Panopticon is a circular prison design where inmates can be watched at any moment from a central tower but cannot see the guard. Inmates internalize surveillance, policing their own behavior out of uncertainty.
usage: Foucault used the Panopticon as a metaphor for modern disciplinary societies (schools, factories, hospitals).
spanish: Michel Foucault analizó cómo las instituciones modernas imponen el cumplimiento y la autovigilancia utilizando el diseño arquitectónico de Bentham del Panóptico (Panopticon).

text: Jacques Derrida developed a critical analysis method to expose the internal contradictions and hierarchies of binary oppositions in texts, known as {{c1::Deconstruction}}.
explanation: Deconstruction challenges logocentrism. It demonstrates that philosophical systems rely on binary pairs (e.g. speech/writing, presence/absence) where one is favored, and seeks to subvert and destabilize these hierarchies.
usage: Deconstruction shows that texts have multiple, unstable, and self-contradictory meanings.
spanish: Jacques Derrida desarrolló un método de análisis crítico para exponer las contradicciones internas y las jerarquías de las oposiciones binarias en los textos, conocido como Deconstrucción.

text: René Descartes sought an indubitable foundation for knowledge, arriving at the primary truth: {{c1::Cogito, ergo sum}} (I think, therefore I am).
explanation: Descartes used methodical doubt to reject all beliefs that could possibly be false. He realized that even if an evil demon is deceiving him, the very act of thinking proves his own existence.
usage: This foundational truth marked the beginning of modern rationalist epistemology.
spanish: René Descartes buscó un fundamento indudable para el conocimiento, llegando a la verdad primaria: Cogito, ergo sum (Pienso, luego existo).

text: David Hume challenged empiricism by questioning if we can logically justify predicting future events based on past observations, known as the {{c1::problem of induction}}.
explanation: The problem of induction states that we cannot prove the uniformity of nature logically. Relying on past experiences to justify future outcomes assumes the very principle we need to prove.
usage: Hume's problem of induction heavily influenced Kant's critique of pure reason.
spanish: David Hume desafió el empirismo al cuestionar si podemos justificar lógicamente la predicción de eventos futuros basados en observaciones pasadas, lo que se conoce como el problema de la inducción.

text: Nietzsche proposed a goal for humanity consisting of self-mastery and the creation of one's own values, embodied in the figure of the {{c1::Übermensch}} (Overman).
explanation: The Übermensch is a person who overcomes the nihilism caused by the death of God by rising above herd morality, constructing their own values, and embracing life fully.
usage: Thus Spoke Zarathustra is the primary text introducing the Übermensch concept.
spanish: Nietzsche propuso una meta para la humanidad que consiste en el autodominio y la creación de los propios valores, encarnada en la figura del Übermensch (Superhombre).

text: Sartre defined the act of adopting false values under social pressure to escape the anxiety of absolute freedom as {{c1::bad faith}} (mauvaise foi).
explanation: Bad faith occurs when a person convinces themselves that they do not have choices, pretending to be a passive object (e.g. playing a role like a waiter too perfectly) to avoid the responsibility of self-definition.
usage: Acting in bad faith is the opposite of living authentically.
spanish: Sartre definió el acto de adoptar falsos valores bajo la presión social para escapar de la ansiedad de la libertad absoluta como mala fe (bad faith).
